sentences.text: [BEEP]
My name in Australia, John Armer, A-R-M-E-R.
Janek, J-A-N-E-K, Armer, A-R-M-E-R.
The Hebrew first name was Jakob, Jakob.
J-A-K-O-B.
No.
26 of nine, 1925.
I didn't have-- I had, you know-- should I share that this is not the right birthday?
On passport documents, 26 of nine, 1925.
But my real birthday was 18th of nine, 1925.
I am 69 and 10 months.
The city was Krakow and the country was Poland.
Krakow is K-R-A-K-O-W.
I had two brothers and one sister.
And about my family, my family, my parents were Orthodox Jews living in Poland.
And my father was a grain and flour merchant before the war.
And my father and his father and his his father was born in Poland for the last three or four generation.
My father's first name was Israel, Israel Mordechai Armer.
He was an Orthodox Jew, yes.
Yes, he did belong to Belzer Hasidim.
They were-- they were strict orthodox, yes.
But not so strict, you know.
My father wasn't so strict.
[LAUGHTER]
Well, all I remember was I went to the-- to the Jewish school in the afternoon, which called Cheder.
Cheder.
And in the morning, I went to the Roman Catholic Polish School.
So before the-- before-- before lunch, I used to learn Polish and all the other secular thing.
In the afternoon, I used to learn Hebrew and the Talmud.
Eh, yes, we like it very much.
We played a little bit soccer.
And we fight with the rabbi, but it was all right.
Well, we always used to, you know, make jokes, you know.
It wasn't so-- it was just like a Cheder, you know.
Everybody did what they wanted.
It wasn't so strict as in the Polish school in the morning.
There was no discipline, in other words.
No strict discipline.
Yes, it was, yes.
And it was enjoyable because you learned-- they had a system where they-- they did not-- they did not press people, the pupils.
They did not press them so much.
So-- so they still-- at the end of the week, they still knew what they had learned from the beginning of the week.
No, it didn't have anything, because it was just a Shteibel.
Do you know what the Shteibel is?
It was just a-- a room, you know.
And plain furniture in it.
And you're sitting next on the-- on the plain chairs, like a bank.
And you learn.
You learn between 2:00 and 6:00.
Four hours a day.
No, there was a Haskel Faiwel.
Faiwel.
Was my teacher, and he was very good.
He was my teacher right through.
F-A-I-W-E-L, Faiwel.
He was one of my best teachers, yes.
My mother did not come from such Orthodox Jews.
My mother came originally from-- from-- from Poland, which-- which used to belong before the war to Germany.
She come from, I can't remember the city.
But anyway, she comes from a-- from a German Jews.
So she-- she was not so strict orthodox.
Yes, my mother first name was Amalia.
Rosenbaum.
R-O-S-E-N-B-A-U-M.
My mother helped my father in the business, yes.
We had a permanent maid.
She was with us for 26 years.
And she brought up all the children.
Yes, Maria.
A good Polish name.
That I wouldn't remember.
That I was not interested in those days.
No.
No.
Well, we always used to call her Maria, because she did everything.
She-- she was in the-- in the household.
She knew all the-- all the rituals in kosher.
So we could rely on her.
And she was good with the children.
I wouldn't remember, because I was the youngest one, so I wouldn't remember whether she was good with the other three children.
But all I know was that we'd had her for-- when I was born, she was already 26 years there.
No, who could afford pets in Poland?
[LAUGHS] No.
No pets, no telephone, no radio, no television.
What else do you want to know?
No cars.
Well, we did, as I told you, after the Cheder we used to play soccer.
There was a very big place over there, next to there, and we used to play soccer.
And then we went to the synagogue for the evening prayer.
And on Saturday we went to the synagogue.
And on Sunday, we went-- Sunday was I had to go to the Cheder all day, because the Polish school was only from Monday till Friday.
Yes, I remember it.
[INAUDIBLE] School.
I was the primer.
I was from the beginning till about sixth class.
Sixth class, actually.
I went through six classes.
The year was the last, it was 1936 or '37, I don't know, but something like that.
Because 1938, I had my bar mitzvah, so that's when I finished the school.
Well, you would have to work out 13 years from '25. '38. '38.
There is no such a bar mitzvah like here in Australia.
We-- my father took a bottle of wine and a-- a bit of a cake, and we went to the synagogue.
And I had to put on the tefillin and everybody was drinking L'Chaim.
And that's it.
That was the whole-- whole bar mitzvah.
And on Saturday, we're called to the Torah.
That's all.
That's all.
That's the bar mitzvah.
And the bar mitzvah was on the day when the bar mitzvah was, like middle of the week [INAUDIBLE].
But it was in the middle of the week.
It was celebrated in the morning prayer in the synagogue.
Well, my-- my first brother was Isaac, the-- the-- the oldest one.
And the-- the-- the-- the younger-- the middle one was Henry, Henrick they called it.
Well, my brother Henrick, he died in concentration camp after the war.
And my brother died a few years ago here in Australia.
Straight after the war.
As a result of the malnutrition and all the sickness.
He survived the war, but he didn't survive the Holocaust, I guess.
My sister went to-- it's at Krakow-- there was a-- they took my sister with my parents to the now what is-- to the concentration camp.
Not concentration camp, but to the gas chambers.
No.
I wouldn't know, but it could be either Treblinka or Auschwitz.
Karola.
Karola.
She was married and she had a child and a husband.
Her married-- Lesman.
L-E-S-M-A-N.
Her husband survived after the war-- till after the war-- because he wasn't-- she-- she was taken because of the child, but he stayed.
And he married again and he live in Israel.
But I believe he died.
He would be now in his 80s.
I have no contact with him anymore at this stage.
Yes, I had with him contact till 1984--
--where I went to visit him.
His first name was Wolf.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
Well, you want to know what happened to them?
They went to the gas chambers.
That's what you want to know.
No, my parents were not in a camp.
My care-- my parents were first in Krakow.
Then we went to Wieliczka.
And from Wieliczka, we went to the-- we had to go to the ghetto in Krakow.
But my father and my mother was never in the concentration camp, never in the work-- they were straight taken away to the gas chambers from ghetto.
Well, my father was 56 and my mother was a little bit younger then, that particular time.
Yeah.
Yes.
Nine, number nine, Rynek Podguorski, Podgorze.
Rynek, R-Y-N-E-K, Podguorski, P-O-D-G-U-O-R-S-K-I.
Podgorze.
P-O-D-G-O-R-Z-E.
It was just-- opposite was the ghetto.
Opposite ghetto.
No, that was outside the ghetto.
It always remained outside the ghetto.
It always remained outside the ghetto.
That's what helped me, because I had the neighbors and I could get some food from there and smuggle into the ghetto during the war.
No, not very much.

It was just a normal routine where we went to school, we went to the Cheder, and we-- we celebrated all the Jewish holidays like Pesach, Shvuot, and-- and what you call it-- Simchat Torah, and all the other things.
But it was not-- not-- and every summer, I used to go for a month or six weeks, to the-- to the country.
I used to go to the parents of-- of-- of Maria, because they had a farm outside.
And that's it.
That was the whole-- we used to go every year there.
We used to go there with her, and we came back after the summer.
Yes.
Not-- no.
She didn't take all of-- take me and my younger bro-- my older brother.
That's all.
And we had a-- we had a good time on the farm with the cows and the horses.
You know, that sort of thing.
That was before the war, now.
My class, there were only three.
And that was they tolerate us, because the school was open on Saturday.
It was from-- from Monday to Saturday.
But-- but the teachers tolerate us because we didn't come on Saturday.
I went to synagogue.
And I used to have a few non-Jewish friends, which I always used to go to them and they told me what was-- what-- what we learned on Sunday-- on Saturday-- and I had to catch up.
It was every week.
No, only to the last two years, were there-- between '35, '36 were the anti-semitism was growing in Poland due to the Hitler in the moment.
But up till then, I didn't feel anything.
No.
No, I didn't notice nothing.
It was-- it was a very short distance from home to the school, walking distance.

Well, there-- there used to be anti-semitism, and particularly my father felt it in the trade, because there was one minister, he used to say-- he got out and he said, his word was you should not hate the Jews.
But what you should do, you should boycott them.
In other words, don't buy off them.
In other words, you understand what I mean?
And that's what we felt.
He said, don't hate them, don't prosecute them, just boycott them.
And that went right through the community.
And we did not-- could do so much trade as of course before those-- before those years.
But we still survive, because the competition-- they still have to come, because we were the cheapest or something like that.
No, it changed sudden.
Up to '39, we didn't-- hardship, we did not feel.
There was plenty to eat, and we sleeped and everyth-- there's no problem.
But the change suddenly, 1939, when the German move into Poland.
In the first few days, the first seven days, they knew.
They had a list.
They knew that we are the merchants.
So they came and they took everything away.
The German took away all the flour and the grain and all the produce.
They took-- they came with a big truck and they took it away.
That's it.
Army Germans.
Army Germans.
They-- they-- they came with a piece of paper and they-- they want to take it.
And we had to sign it that we get the money for it, which we never did.
In other words, the business went flat.
If you have no goods to sell, what-- how can you conduct a business?
Little bit of business.
We used to buy on the black market and sell on the black market, because the business was still there.
Well, we-- we did manage.
We managed.
We managed.
Up to the ghetto, we managed.
But I was-- I didn't go into it, because that wasn't my department.
Department belonged to my father and Maria.
But Maria left in 1940. 1940-- As soon the things get too hot, she left.
What?
The department.
The departm-- the kosher department, the food, sh-- they were looking after it.
Not the apartment, no.
No, the department.
The food area.
The food area, that's right.
So I couldn't tell you.
Yes, I saw it, yes.
They were not only satis-- they were not satisfied as they-- as they-- confiscated all the grain and the flour and even the salt and the sugar from the-- from the shop.
But they went as far as went to the premises and they went to where we live to see whether we didn't hide anything.
Yes, they were German soldiers, yes.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that was the first-- first week of-- first, second week during-- in the beginning of the war.
They didn't give anybody a chance to sell it or to hide it, you know.
Yes, yes.
Happened right through it.
What could he do?
He couldn't do very much.
He-- if you would-- if you would resist them, they-- they had-- they had guns with them, you know.
How could you resist it?
You were lucky that they leave you alone.
There was no resistance.
You couldn't do-- you couldn't resist it.
You couldn't demand anything.
The only thing my father did, after the first raid, they left something.
So what he did, we had a hiding place where-- behind the sh-- behind the premises somewhere, where it was a-- a room where you couldn't-- you couldn't get in.
They-- nobody knew about the room.
And that's where we hide a lot of food and produce for ourselves, not for selling purposes, you know.
So we were- we were lucky that we could still-- there was no bread available, so we had flour.
We baked the bread, you know.
That was September.
It went on till 1940, the beginning of '41.
That's when we went to Velichka.
He was carrying on very limited, limited.
There was nothing for sale, but he still managed to buy something and to sell, because we had a lot of contacts.
A lot of-- a lot of the Polish peasant were-- what you call it-- they had-- they used to buy from us on credit.
And there was-- he had a lot of-- they had a lot of credit with us, which they didn't pay.
So they gave us something, you know, to keep us going.
So it wasn't-- we could survive.
We could survive, like I said, to the end of the war, if they wouldn't do anything.
Because we could alwa-- being in the same place, you could always help yourself somehow.
But then, we went to Velichka.
And from Velichka, you could not carry all the things.
So the only thing which we carried is the money.
The money and gold and jewelry, which my father still had it.
And that was converted in Velichka into the food.
We lived from it.
Well, the Jewish reaction was that-- that-- that it's not going to be good under the Germans.
That-- that they-- all the time, they want something else.
They were not satisfied only with confiscated your produce.
Later they want furs because the-- the war started.
They were preparing the war against Russia.
So everybody had to give the furs.
We're not allowed to have any furs.
And you know, before the war, the Jewish people had a lot of furs.
The families, the mother and the sister.
So everything has to be taken away.
All the jewelry has to be-- everything has to-- you had to give it.
But of course, we didn't.
We give some of it, not-- not all of it.
When did they start asking for this materials, the--
Oh, straight, maybe a month or six weeks after.
So already in October, November of '39.
That's right.
They demanded things, you see.
As much as they could, you know, as much as they could.
Where they didn't cooperate-- I mean, with the furs, they had to.
They knew-- there was a big pile, you know, was a pile as maybe 10-- 10 story pile of furs where they collected.
And then later they converted into for the soldiers.
You're not allowed to have any furs.
And it was a hard life.
It wasn't as good as before, but nobody starved.
Still I say, I still my-- I mean, in our family, we did not starve.
Where can you live?
You can't live.
It is all-- you can't-- you couldn't live from Krakow to the-- to Warsaw, you couldn't live.
You had to have papers, permission.
From the German authorities.
You couldn't board a train.
You couldn't board a train.
My auntie was living about just like from here to Katoomba, [INAUDIBLE], which is not far from Auschwitz.
You could not board a train without the permission.
The German soldier were on the station.
And you have to have a-- a pass.
Because they introduced the-- the, what you call it, in the first few months they introduce it.
You had to have, what you call-- what is it called?
Armband, yes, with the star.
No, no.
A little bit later, little bit later.
Something like that, yes.
You had to have it.
White with a blue-- blue Magen David.
Star of David, yes.
And you could not-- if they catch you without it, you would be in a big trouble.
While we're in Krakow, you didn't have to have papers when you were in this surrounding.
But you couldn't move.
You say you leave Poland.
How can you leave Poland?
You can't live anywhere.
And all the borders were closed.
That's right, yes.
No, they were talking about building the ghetto.
They did eventually, you know.
And that was the last thing was they did it.
Because-- because before they build the ghetto, the Jewish people used to live everywhere in Krakow.
In [?
Platz, ?] in Padgorze, anywhere.
Yes, there was Judenrat, they called it.
Jewish, there was the-- like a Jewish wel-- not welfare.
You call it here welfare, but over there it was Judenrat.
That was the president--
The Jewish council.
A Jewish council.
Yes.
President and all the other thing.
I would not remember.
All I remember is was one of them was Simcha Spira.
Simcha Spira-- how do you spell it?
S-I-M-C-H-A, Spira, S-P-I-R-A.
He was the-- he was the president of the ghetto.
That I remember.
But that was later on.
That was later on.
But before that, I can't remember.
There was one-- oh yes.
There was one by the name of Rubinstein, R-U-B-I-N-S-T-E-I-N.
He was, before the war, a teacher.
And he was, I think, president or something like that.
Yes, from the beginning.
They formed the Judenrat straightaway, as soon as the German came in.
Because they could not go to individual Jew what they wanted.
They went to the Judenrat and the Judenrat went to the Jews and demanded what-- whatever they wanted.
No.
Well, they-- they went to the Judenrat, and they tell them that they want that, and that day all the Jews should wear the armband.
So the Judenrat went and he put up-- put up all the sign.
And-- and announce it, and that's it.
There was not a radio announce or television.
It was just they had paper, you know.
Posted on every corner, yeah.
Well, that's where they demand the furs, so they did it the same way.
They posted and everybody had to go get.
Or they want jewelry.
Right from the beginning, the first, they said anybody who would be find with a weapon would be shot on the spot.
That was right-- I've forgotten to tell you-- that's right from the beginning.
So first it was they take care of the weapon.
And then they take care of the food.
Then they took care of the furs.
Then they took care of the jewelry.
Systematically, they make the Jews poorer and poorer.
And then when they make them poor, then they shift them from one place to another till they brought them to ghetto.
And then they exterminate them from the ghetto to there-- it was a-- it was a organized, systematical-- systematic extermination, put it this way.
Not if I know, but-- nobody had-- not-- not-- not what I know had anybody weapons.
No, no way.
They wouldn't jeopardize the family.
No, but even before the Germans came.
No, nobody had a weapon.
Well, we left Krakow and we went to Velichka.
My family.
We didn't go because we-- we went out on the [? cart, ?] because my-- my-- my father's brother, with the children, were living there.
Uncle, yes.
Yes, Jakob Armer.
He was a younger brother.
He had 11 children.
Yes, we went there because this uncle had only one son.
And when the German came into Velichka, right from the beginning, the first few weeks, they rounded up 50 Jews.
And between the 50 Jews was my uncle and his older son.
And they took them outside Velichka and they shot them for no reason.
So there was just the auntie with the children left and a business to run.
So my father, we went over there together, you know, to help them as much as we could.
Well, my younger brother, because my older brother was somewhere else.
And my sister, her husband, and a child.
Something like that.
That I wouldn't know because-- when was the liquid-- the liquida-- before the liquidation of the ghetto, we came back.
Would be end of-- we were there approximately 12 months.
He went to-- he went to his brother's-- his widow-- the brothers' wife business.
But there was not enough business over there.
So he used to come-- he used to travel every day from Velichka to Krakow to look after his business, because we still have the business over there.
We didn't live over there, but the business was still there.
We live Velichka.
And he used to-- And he used to go in the morning, come back in the evening.
He used to make some business over there.
He used to get some fruit and some other things from the peasant and used to sell it.
And some of them he was to brought back to-- to Krakow-- to Velichka.
Yes, we lived like free people.
We even attend to the synagogue.
There was a synagogue over there.
Yes, still open, yes, still allowed.
It was open, but you had to be careful when you go in.
You had to be careful in Velichka also.
They didn't know where the synagogue-- synagogue was somewhere, you know, on the side street where nobody knew it.
No, I don't know anything.
I left Velichka a long time ago.
I left Velichka before the liquidation of Krakow.
No, I wouldn't remember it.
Because we went-- we went like a freelance.
We were now on a [? cart, ?] you know.
Right.
No, but I understand that Jews were taken from Krakow to Velichka.
Some of them, yes.
But not-- we went-- we went early.
Then we went back from Velichka to Krakow.
Because Veli--
I know from another interview--
